citations.narrative:
At the time of inspection, no record of acquisition was available for review regarding two gibbons. Record keeping allows
Animal Care Personnel to evaluate compliance with the AWA Regulations and Standards. Every dealer other than
operators of auction sales and brokers to whom animals are consigned, and exhibitor shall make, keep, and maintain
records or forms which fully and correctly disclose the following information concerning animals other than dogs and cats,
purchased or otherwise acquired, owned, held, leased, or otherwise in his or her possession or under his or her control, or
which is transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise disposed of by that dealer or exhibitor.
To be corrected from this time forward.

A male spider monkey was observed with several lesions on the digits of the front and rear feet. The wounds were open
with red tissue visible beneath the missing skin. Some areas of the digits had detached skin remaining around the
affected digits. The majority of the tail was absent with only an open, red, very short stump remaining. The facility
associated the injuries with very cold weather during the last week of January 2026. The attending veterinarian and
medical records confirmed the injury resulted from exposure to cold temperatures. The animal was housed in sheltered
housing with access to a heat source, but the facility failed to monitor to ensure exposure to extreme temperatures did not
occur. Weather records document that the low temperatures for that area on January 24 was 22°F, 16°F on January 25,
13°F on January 26, 14°F on January 27, and 20°F on January 28, 2026. Exposing nonhuman primates to temperatures
below 45°F is not in accordance with generally accepted husbandry practices, and may contribute to adverse effects,
including death.
When climatic conditions present a threat to an animal’s health or well-being, appropriate measures must be taken to
alleviate the impact of those conditions. An animal may never be subjected to any combination of temperature, humidity,
and time that is detrimental to the animal’s health or well-being, taking into consideration such factors as the animal’s age,
species, breed, overall health status, and acclimation.
To be corrected before the onset of the next cold spell, no later than October 1, 2026.

At the time of inspection, the facility reported that a male capuchin monkey had escaped from his primary enclosure for a
period of approximately four days during the fall of 2025. The animal was recovered from a location outside the official
premises. The animal was safe when it was recovered and no members of the public were harmed.
Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they contain the nonhuman primates securely and
prevent accidental opening of the enclosure, including opening by the animal.
To be corrected from this time forward.

The outdoor enclosure housing one prairie dog contained no adequate shelter for protection from climatic conditions. A
torn cloth hammock that was wet from the previous rain was the only shelter provided. The enclosure had been
constructed to house non-human primates and was not modified to provide shelter to the prairie dog.
Natural or artificial shelter appropriate to the local climatic conditions for the species concerned shall be provided for all
animals kept outdoors to afford them protection and to prevent discomfort to such animals.
Corrected at time of inspection.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
At the time of inspection, no record of acquisition was available for review regarding two gibbons. Record keeping allows 
Animal Care Personnel to evaluate compliance with the AWA Regulations and Standards. Every dealer other than 
operators of auction sales and brokers to whom animals are consigned, and exhibitor shall make, keep, and maintain 
records or forms which fully and correctly disclose the following information concerning animals other than dogs and cats, 
purchased or otherwise acquired, owned, held, leased, or otherwise in his or her possession or under his or her control, or 
which is transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise disposed of by that dealer or exhibitor.  
 
To be corrected from this time forward. 
2.131(e)  Critical
   
Handling of animals. 
A male spider monkey was observed with several lesions on the digits of the front and rear feet. The wounds were open 
with red tissue visible beneath the missing skin. Some areas of the digits had detached skin remaining around the 
affected digits. The majority of the tail was absent with only an open, red, very short stump remaining. The facility 
associated the injuries with very cold weather during the last week of January 2026. The attending veterinarian and 
medical records confirmed the injury resulted from exposure to cold temperatures. The animal was housed in sheltered 
housing with access to a heat source, but the facility failed to monitor to ensure exposure to extreme temperatures did not 
occur. Weather records document that the low temperatures for that area on January 24 was 22°F, 16°F on January 25, 
13°F on January 26, 14°F on January 27, and 20°F on January 28, 2026. Exposing nonhuman primates to temperatures 
below 45°F is not in accordance with generally accepted husbandry practices, and may contribute to adverse effects, 
including death. 
 
When climatic conditions present a threat to an animal’s health or well-being, appropriate measures must be taken to 
alleviate the impact of those conditions. An animal may never be subjected to any combination of temperature, humidity, 
and time that is detrimental to the animal’s health or well-being, taking into consideration such factors as the animal’s age, 
species, breed, overall health status, and acclimation. 
 
To be corrected before the onset of the next cold spell, no later than October 1, 2026.

3.80(a)(2)(iii) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
At the time of inspection, the facility reported that a male capuchin monkey had escaped from his primary enclosure for a 
period of approximately four days during the fall of 2025. The animal was recovered from a location outside the official 
premises. The animal was safe when it was recovered and no members of the public were harmed. 
 
Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they contain the nonhuman primates securely and 
prevent accidental opening of the enclosure, including opening by the animal. 
 
To be corrected from this time forward. 
3.127(b) 
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
The outdoor enclosure housing one prairie dog contained no adequate shelter for protection from climatic conditions. A 
torn cloth hammock that was wet from the previous rain was the only shelter provided. The enclosure had been 
constructed to house non-human primates and was not modified to provide shelter to the prairie dog.  
 
Natural or artificial shelter appropriate to the local climatic conditions for the species concerned shall be provided for all 
animals kept outdoors to afford them protection and to prevent discomfort to such animals. 
 
Corrected at time of inspection. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
